behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) in the contract
behaviors.justification: A mock extractor that returns hardcoded terms would pass any unit test, but would hide the real failure: the LLM's inability to extract terms from a given contract text. The extractor's correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces—it could miss subtle clauses, misinterpret dates, or fail on certain document formats. Only a real LLM call against actual contract text can verify the extraction is complete and accurate.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real clause extraction reviewer with a legal contract containing multiple key terms, and verify the output includes all expected parties, key dates (effective date, renewal date, termination date), core obligations, and penalty clauses without missing any required elements.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags problematic language under the configured policy, even when phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: Detecting euphemistic or indirect risk language requires sophisticated language understanding that only a real LLM can provide. A mocked risk assessor that returns a fixed flag list would always pass, hiding the real challenge: whether the LLM can recognize that phrases like 'at the sole discretion of' or 'commercially reasonable' actually mask high-risk delegation or undefined standards. The success of this behavior is entirely dependent on the LLM's reasoning, not on code logic.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real risk assessment reviewer with contracts containing indirect or euphemistic problematic language (e.g., vague indemnification, discretionary enforcement clauses) and the configured policy, then verify that risky clauses are flagged even when the risk is not explicitly stated.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance is jurisdiction-specific and nuanced. A mocked checker could always return 'compliant' or 'non-compliant' without actually understanding the regulations. The real failure would be: the LLM either misunderstands jurisdiction-specific requirements (e.g., UK GDPR vs. California privacy law) or fails to identify which regulations apply. Only calling the real LLM with actual contract text and jurisdiction configuration can verify correct compliance determination.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real compliance checker with a contract and a specific jurisdiction configuration, then verify that the determination correctly identifies whether all applicable regulatory requirements (tax withholding, data protection, labor laws, industry-specific rules) are met for that jurisdiction.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments, the merge step preserves both assessments with clear attribution to each reviewer
behaviors.justification: The merge logic itself is deterministic, but testing it in isolation with perfectly mocked inputs could hide real failures: (1) the three independent reviewers may identify and phrase the same clause differently, making clause-matching fail; (2) reviewer output structures may be inconsistent or unexpected; (3) attribution metadata from real reviewers could be absent or malformed. To catch these real failures, you need to see how actual reviewer modules structure their outputs and verify the merge logic handles realistic variations in output format and clause identification.
behaviors.test_description: Run the three independent reviewers against a contract where they naturally produce conflicting assessments of the same clause (e.g., extractor calls it 'standard indemnity', risk assessor flags it as 'high risk'). Verify the merged output includes both assessments, clearly attributes each to its source reviewer, and preserves all information without losing details.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This is a multi-step agentic sequence: three independent LLM calls (clause extraction, risk assessment, compliance check) must all complete, their outputs collected correctly, and merged without data loss. A mock of any step would hide failures in: real LLM timeouts, partial failures (one reviewer succeeds, another times out), format mismatches between real outputs, or real parallelization issues. The pipeline's correctness depends on the actual behavior of all three LLM-based reviewers working together, not on any single step in isolation. Only running the full workflow with real LLM calls can verify the orchestration works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the complete pipeline on a real legal contract: dispatch all three reviewers in parallel, collect each result, wait for all to complete, merge into a unified report. Verify the report is complete with no individual reviewer findings omitted, all conflicts are preserved with attribution, and the entire process handles reasonable contract sizes and LLM latencies without data loss.
